summary: Jeff Atwood reviews Jakob Nielsen's list of top ten blog design mistakes and largely agrees with the advice, but argues that Nielsen missed the biggest mistake of all: disabling comments. He contends that true blogging requires two-way communication between author and reader, otherwise it's just traditional publishing. Atwood admits to violating several of the guidelines himself, including not having an author photo, which caused problems when trying to meet other bloggers at a conference. The post's title is a self-deprecating joke referencing Nielsen's observation that only people with too much time on their hands read blogs that cover many topics.
key_points: Disabling comments is the biggest blog design mistake that Nielsen's list overlooked
Two-way communication between author and reader is what distinguishes blogging from traditional publishing
Authors must actually read and respond to comments for it to count as real communication
Having an author photo matters for real-world recognition at conferences and events
Covering too many topics attracts only low-value readers who have excessive free time
money_quotes: You don't have a blog until you allow public, two way communication between the author and the reader.
And when I say communication, I mean it: the author has to actually read and even (gasp!) respond to the comments.
Otherwise you're just publishing content, like every other newspaper since the printing press was invented. Meh.
The only people who read everything are those with too much time on their hands (a low-value demographic).
I certainly regretted not having my picture on my blog when I went to PDC 2005 and belatedly realized it was virtually impossible for me to meet any other bloggers.
key_insight: Blogging's defining characteristic is two-way communication with readers—without comments and author engagement, you're just another old-media publisher.
